cases.name: Michigan Dept. of State Police v. Sitz
cases.cite: Michigan Dept. of State Police v. Sitz, 496 U.S. 444 (1990)
cases.holding: Suspicionless sobriety (DUI) checkpoints are constitutional; the state's interest in combating drunk driving and the checkpoint's effectiveness outweigh the brief, minimal intrusion on motorists.

cases.name: City of Indianapolis v. Edmond
cases.cite: City of Indianapolis v. Edmond, 531 U.S. 32 (2000)
cases.holding: A checkpoint program whose primary purpose is to detect ordinary criminal wrongdoing / general crime control (here, drug interdiction) is unconstitutional; suspicionless checkpoints require a purpose beyond the general interest in crime control.

quotes.text: Because the primary purpose of the Indianapolis narcotics checkpoint program is to uncover evidence of ordinary criminal wrongdoing, the program contravenes the Fourth Amendment.

cases.name: Illinois v. Lidster
cases.holding: An information-seeking checkpoint — stopping motorists to ask whether they witnessed an earlier crime committed by *someone else* — is constitutional; it is not aimed at determining the stopped drivers' own wrongdoing.

cases.name: New Jersey v. T.L.O.
cases.holding: A public-school official may search a student without a warrant or probable cause; the search need only be reasonable at its inception and reasonable in scope under the circumstances.

cases.name: Board of Education v. Earls
cases.cite: Board of Education v. Earls, 536 U.S. 822 (2002)
cases.holding: Suspicionless drug testing of all students participating in competitive extracurricular activities is a reasonable special-needs search.

cases.name: Griffin v. Wisconsin
cases.holding: A warrantless search of a probationer's home pursuant to a valid regulation is reasonable when supported by "reasonable grounds"; probation is a special need permitting departure from the warrant/PC requirement.

cases.name: Samson v. California
cases.holding: A suspicionless search of a parolee is reasonable; a parolee subject to a search condition has severely diminished privacy expectations, so officers need no individualized suspicion.

cases.name: Camara v. Municipal Court
cases.holding: Administrative inspections of private property generally require a warrant, but it may be an "area warrant" issued on reasonable legislative/administrative standards rather than individualized probable cause.

cases.name: Ferguson v. City of Charleston
cases.cite: Ferguson v. City of Charleston, 532 U.S. 67 (2001)
cases.holding: Covertly drug-testing pregnant patients and reporting results to law enforcement is an unreasonable search; the special-needs exception does NOT apply when the program's immediate objective is to generate evidence for law enforcement.

cases.name: Chandler v. Miller
cases.holding: Georgia's suspicionless drug-testing requirement for candidates for state office is unconstitutional — there was no concrete, special need beyond a symbolic show of commitment to the war on drugs.

cases.name: Maryland v. King
cases.holding: Taking a buccal (cheek) DNA swab from a person arrested for a serious offense and held in custody is a reasonable booking procedure under the Fourth Amendment.
cases.treatment_note: Current; DNA cheek-swab of arrestees for serious offenses is a reasonable booking procedure. ~670 cites, no negative treatment.

cases.name: City of Los Angeles v. Patel
cases.cite: City of Los Angeles v. Patel, 576 U.S. 409 (2015)
cases.holding: A hotel guest-registry inspection ordinance is facially unconstitutional because it gives operators no opportunity for pre-compliance review before an impartial decisionmaker; hotels are not a closely regulated industry.

cases.name: New York v. Burger
cases.holding: A warrantless administrative inspection of a closely (pervasively) regulated business — here, an automobile junkyard — is reasonable if there's a substantial government interest, the inspection is necessary to the regulatory scheme, and the statute provides a constitutionally adequate substitute for a warrant.

cases.name: South Dakota v. Opperman
cases.holding: An inventory search of a lawfully impounded vehicle conducted pursuant to standard police procedures, and not as a pretext concealing an investigatory motive, is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment.

quotes.text: there is no suggestion whatever that this standard procedure ... was a pretext concealing an investigatory police motive.
in following standard police procedures ... the conduct of the police was not “unreasonable” under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.name: Colorado v. Bertine
cases.holding: Inventory searches (including opening closed containers) are permissible where police discretion is exercised according to standardized criteria and on the basis of something other than suspicion of evidence of criminal activity; inventories must be conducted per standardized criteria.

quotes.text: [Lafayette] prohibits the exercise of police discretion so long as that discretion is exercised according to standard criteria and on the basis of something other than suspicion of evidence of criminal activity.
Our decisions have always adhered to the requirement that inventories be conducted according to standardized criteria.

cases.name: Florida v. Wells
cases.holding: An inventory search must not be a ruse for general rummaging to discover incriminating evidence; standardized criteria or established routine must govern the opening of containers. The policy must be designed to produce an inventory, and the officer must not have unbridled latitude.

quotes.text: an inventory search must not be a ruse for a general rummaging in order to discover incriminating evidence. The policy or practice governing inventory searches should be designed to produce an inventory.
The individual police officer must not be allowed so much latitude that inventory searches are turned into “a purposeful and general means of discovering evidence of crime.”

cases.name: Vernonia School District 47J v. Acton
cases.cite: Vernonia School District 47J v. Acton, 515 U.S. 646 (1995)
cases.holding: Suspicionless random drug testing of public-school student athletes is reasonable under the special-needs doctrine, given athletes' reduced privacy expectations and the school's interest in deterring drug use.

cases.name: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n
cases.cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602, 614 (1989)

cases.name: National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab
cases.cite: National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab, 489 U.S. 656 (1989)
cases.holding: Suspicionless drug testing of Customs employees seeking drug-interdiction or firearm-carrying positions is reasonable under the special-needs doctrine.

cases.name: New Jersey v. T.L.O.
cases.holding: A school official's search of a student requires only reasonableness under all the circumstances — justified at inception + reasonably related in scope — not a warrant or probable cause.

quotes.text: Determining the reasonableness of any search involves a twofold inquiry: first, one must consider "whether the . . . action was justified at its inception," . . . second, one must determine whether the search as actually conducted "was reasonably related in scope to the circumstances which justified the interference in the first place".

cases.name: United States v. Knights
cases.holding: A warrantless search of a probationer subject to a search condition, supported by reasonable suspicion, is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment even where the purpose is investigatory/law-enforcement.

quotes.text: We therefore hold that the warrantless search of Knights, supported by reasonable suspicion and authorized by a condition of probation, was reasonable within the meaning of the Fourth Amendment.

cases.treatment_note: Current; reasonable-suspicion probation-search rule (cf. Samson v. California for parolees/suspicionless).

note: Phase-3 FINAL authoring manifest: packet(manifest+deck) + P3 gap verifications + marquee quotes + good-law.
